purpose: Activate M5 "all scales" knowledge. Confirm students can read graphs with scale of 2 (only appearance in M6).
visual: Bar graph appears (horizontal, scale of 2, 4 categories: Soccer 12, Basketball 7, Tennis 6, Swimming 11). Title: "After-School Activities"
guide: "Let's warm up your graph skills. Here's a bar graph—check the scale. How many students chose soccer?"
on_correct: "12 students. Scale of 2—each mark on the axis shows 2 students."
on_incorrect: (A - counted bars) "Check the scale. Each axis mark is 2 students, not 1. Count by 2s."
on_incorrect_2: (B or D) "Look at the soccer bar and count by 2s using the scale."
design_note: This is the ONLY scale-of-2 graph in M6. Brief, successful reading activates M5 knowledge.

purpose: Activate M1 comparison language: "how many more" = subtract. This is the foundation for Lesson's two-step strategy.
visual: Same bar graph remains (Soccer 12, Basketball 7, Tennis 6, Swimming 11).
guide: "Now a question about this data. How many more students chose soccer than tennis?"
prompt: "How many more students chose soccer than tennis?"
on_correct: "6 more. 'How many more' means compare—we subtract. Soccer is 12, tennis is 6, difference is 6."
on_incorrect_1: (A - added) "'How many more' means compare, not combine. Subtract to find the difference."
on_incorrect_2: (B or D) "Soccer is 12, tennis is 6. 'How many more' means find the difference: 12 minus 6."
design_note: Explicit callback to M1 language. The phrase "'how many more' means compare—we subtract" should echo in Lesson Section 1.

purpose: Activate scale-of-5 reading while introducing the context students will see again in Lesson 2.1-2.2.
visual: Bar graph appears (vertical, scale of 5, 4 categories: "Favorite Lunch"). Values: Pizza 45, Tacos 25, Salad 10, Burgers 15.
design_note: This context (Favorite Lunch) returns in Lesson 2.1-2.2. Students will recognize it when the two-step question appears.

purpose: Tease the exact question students will work through in Lesson 2.2. Creates anticipation with a promise of resolution.
visual: Same bar graph remains (Pizza 45, Tacos 25, Salad 10, Burgers 15). Question appears: "How many more students chose pizza than tacos and salad together?"
guide: "You've got graph reading down. You've got comparison down. But look at THIS question..."
guide_2: (reading question) "How many more students chose pizza than tacos and salad together?"
guide_3: "See that word 'together'? This question needs more than one step. We'll come back to THIS question in the lesson—and you'll be able to answer it."
design_note: The exact question from W.4 becomes the worked example in Lesson 2.2. Students experience the full arc: "I couldn't answer this → Guide showed me how → Now I can."
